perturbation.original_text: (ii) all offers of Securities by us in the United States have been effected by the U.S. Affiliate in accordance with all applicable U.S. federal and state broker-dealer requirements;
perturbation.changed_text: (ii) all offers of Securities by us in the United States have been effected by the U.S. Affiliate in general accordance with U.S. federal and state broker-dealer requirements, as the U.S. Affiliate reasonably interprets them;
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'in general accordance' and 'as the U.S. Affiliate reasonably interprets them' introduces significant ambiguity. It allows the U.S. Affiliate to potentially deviate from strict compliance based on their own interpretation of the regulations, which weakens the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 15(a)(1) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 requires broker-dealers to register with the SEC and comply with applicable regulations. The modified text allows the U.S. Affiliate to operate based on its own interpretation, potentially avoiding strict compliance with registration and other regulatory requirements, which directly contradicts the mandatory nature of the Exchange Act.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 2B / § 78o Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC 78o : Registration and regulation of brokers and dealers Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 15-COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 2B-SECURITIES EXCHANGES Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous References In Text Amendments Effective Date §78 o . Registration and regulation of brokers and dealers (a) Registration of all persons utilizing exchange facilities to effect transactions; exemptions (1) It shall be unlawful for any broker or dealer which is either a person other than a natural person or a natural person not associated with a broker or dealer which is a person other than a natural person (other than such a broker or dealer whose bu
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating that the U.S. Affiliate must act "in general accordance" with broker-dealer requirements, "as the U.S. Affiliate reasonably interprets them." This weakens the original obligation of strict compliance. Section 15(a)(1) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 requires broker-dealers to register with the SEC and comply with applicable regulations. The law snippet 1, 15 USC 78o, states that "It shall be unlawful for any broker or dealer which is either a person other than a natural person or a natural person not associated with a broker or dealer which is a person other than a natural person..." effectively mandating registration and compliance. The change in contract language, allowing the affiliate to interpret regulations and comply only generally, contradicts the explicit requirement for all broker-dealers to comply, creating a legal contradiction because it undermines a core legal requirement.

perturbation.original_text: (iv) no form of Directed Selling Efforts, General Solicitation or General Advertising was used by us in connection with the offer of the Securities by us in the United States, and we have not acted in any manner involving public offering within the meaning of Section 4(a) (2) of the U.S. Securities Act in connection with the offer of the Securities by us in the United States;
perturbation.changed_text: (iv) To the best of our knowledge, no form of Directed Selling Efforts, General Solicitation or General Advertising was intentionally used by us in connection with the offer of the Securities by us in the United States, and we have not knowingly acted in any manner involving public offering within the meaning of Section 4(a) (2) of the U.S. Securities Act in connection with the offer of the Securities by us in the United States;
perturbation.explanation: Adding "to the best of our knowledge" and "intentionally" and "knowingly" introduces a loophole, making it difficult to prove a violation even if Directed Selling Efforts or General Solicitation occurred. It shifts the burden of proof and allows for plausible deniability.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 502(c) of Regulation D under the Securities Act of 1933 strictly prohibits general solicitation or general advertising in connection with offers and sales under Rule 506. The modified text weakens this prohibition by making it dependent on the Agent's knowledge and intent, which undermines the objective standard of the rule and allows for violations to occur as long as the agent can claim they were not aware of it or it was unintentional. The law requires an outright ban on these activities regardless of intent.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 230.502 - General conditions to be met. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 230.502 - General conditions to be met. CFR prev | next § 230.502 General conditions to be met. The following conditions shall be applicable to offers and sales made under Regulation D ( § 230.500 et seq. of this chapter): (a) Integration. To determine whether offers and sales should be integrated, see § 230.152 . (b) Information requirements —(1) When information must be furnished. If the issuer sells securities under § 230.506(b) to any purchaser that is not an accredited investor , the issuer shall furnish the information specified in paragraph (b)(2) of this section to such purchaser a reasonable time prior to sale. The issuer is not required to furnish the specified information to purchasers when it sells securities under § 230.504, or to any accredited investor . Note: When an issuer provides information to investors pursuant to paragraph (b)(1), it should consider providing such information to accredited investors as well, in view of the anti-fraud provisions of the federal securities laws. (2) Type of information to be furnished. (i) If the issuer is not subject to the reporting requirements of section 13 or 15(d) of the Exchange Act , at a reasonable time prior to the sale of securities the issuer shall furnish to the purchaser, to the extent material to an understanding of the issuer , its business and the securities being offered: (A) Non-financial statement information. If the issuer is eligible to use Regulation A ( § 230.251-263 ), the same kind of information as would be required in Part II of Form 1-A ( § 239.90 of this chapter). If the issuer is not eligible to use Regulation A, the same kind of information as required in Part I of a registration statement filed under the Securities Act on the form that the issuer would be entitled to use. (B) Financial statement infor
perturbation.justification: The original contract language strictly prohibits Directed Selling Efforts, General Solicitation or General Advertising. The changed text introduces the qualifiers "to the best of our knowledge," "intentionally," and "knowingly." This creates a loophole, making it more difficult to prove a violation of Regulation D, Rule 502(c), as highlighted by the Law Explanation, because the agent could claim they were unaware or their actions were unintentional. This weakens the objective standard of the original clause. Scraped law snippets don't directly address the knowledge or intent of actors in the rule, but the explanation strongly suggests that the altered text allows for violations to occur as long as the agent claims ignorance or lack of intent, thereby undermining the outright ban.

perturbation.original_text: (vi) the Agent represents and warrants that with respect to Offered Shares to be sold in reliance on Rule 506(b) of Regulation D, none of it, the U.S. Affiliate, any of its or the U.S. Affiliate's directors, executive officers, general partners, managing members or other officers participating in the Offering, or any other person associated with the Agent who will receive, directly or indirectly, remuneration for solicitation of Subscribers of Offered Shares pursuant to Rule 506(b) of Regulation D (each, a "Dealer Covered Person" and, together, "Dealer Covered Persons"), is subject to any Disqualification Event except for a Disqualification Event (i) covered by Rule 506(d)(2)(i) of Regulation D and (ii) a description of which has been furnished in writing to the Corporation prior to the date hereof;
perturbation.changed_text: (vi) the Agent represents and warrants that with respect to Offered Shares to be sold in reliance on Rule 506(b) of Regulation D, it has conducted a reasonable inquiry and, based on that inquiry, believes that none of it, the U.S. Affiliate, any of its or the U.S. Affiliate's directors, executive officers, general partners, managing members or other officers participating in the Offering, or any other person associated with the Agent who will receive, directly or indirectly, remuneration for solicitation of Subscribers of Offered Shares pursuant to Rule 506(b) of Regulation D (each, a "Dealer Covered Person" and, together, "Dealer Covered Persons"), is subject to any Disqualification Event except for a Disqualification Event (i) covered by Rule 506(d)(2)(i) of Regulation D and (ii) a description of which has been furnished in writing to the Corporation prior to the date hereof;
perturbation.explanation: Changing the representation to be based on a 'reasonable inquiry' and adding 'believes' weakens the absolute assurance required by Rule 506(d). A 'reasonable inquiry' is subjective and less stringent than a guarantee that no disqualification exists. This introduces ambiguity regarding the level of diligence required.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 506(d) of Regulation D creates specific disqualification events that would prevent reliance on the exemption. The agent must comply with these rules without exception. By saying that the agent has simply done a 'reasonable inquiry', that introduces ambiguity and directly contradicts the spirit and letter of the regulation.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 230.506 - Exemption for limited offers and sales without regard to dollar amount of offering. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 230.506 - Exemption for limited offers and sales without regard to dollar amount of offering. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 230.506 Exemption for limited offers and sales without regard to dollar amount of offering. (a) Exemption. Offers and sales of securities by an issuer that satisfy the conditions in paragraph (b) or (c) of this section shall be deemed to be transactions not involving any public offering within the meaning of section 4(a)(2) of the Act . (b) Conditions to be met in offerings subject to limitation on manner of offering —(1) General conditions. To qualify for an exemption under this section, offers and sales must satisfy all the terms and conditions of §§ 230.501 and 230.502. (2) Specific conditions — (i) Limitation on number of purchasers. There are no more than, or the issuer reasonably believes that there are no more than, 35 purchasers of securities from the issuer in offerings under this section in any 90-calendar-day period. Note 1 to paragraph (b)(2)(i): See § 230.501(e) for the calculation of the number of purchasers and § 230.502(a) for what may or may not constitute an offering under paragraph (b) of this section. (ii) Nature of purchasers. Each purchaser who is not an accredited investor either alone or with his purchaser representative(s) has such knowledge and experience in financial and business matters that he is capable of evaluating the merits and risks of the prospective investment, or the issuer reasonably believes immediately prior to making any sale that such purchaser comes within this description. (c) Conditions to be met in offerings not subject to limitation on manner of offering —(1) General conditions. To qualify for exemption under this section, sales must satisfy all the ter
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity by changing the representation and warranty to be based on a 'reasonable inquiry' and adding 'believes.' The original text represents and warrants without these qualifiers, suggesting a higher level of assurance. The change weakens the obligation, potentially conflicting with the implicit requirement for strict compliance often associated with securities regulations like Rule 506(d), as suggested in the Law Explanation, which states that the agent must comply with these rules without exception, and the modified text introduces ambiguity. Though the scraped snippets don't explicitly mention the level of assurance required, the Law Explanation emphasizes that the ambiguity directly contradicts the regulation.
